text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a splenic flexure resection laparoscopic patient with inflammatory bowel disease and had chronic intermittent abdominal pain and bloating. No true obstructive symptoms, but just food intolerance and massive bloating. CAT scans showed a potential stricture just at the descending colon portion of the splenic flexure. and this was corroborated on a colonoscopy. It was able to be traversed. Mucosa of the entire colon was completely normal. The patient just simply had a proximal descending colon stricture and she was selected for resection.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure using three laparoscopic ports: an umbilical 12mm trocar, a left upper quadrant port, and a suprapubic port. The stricture is visible, and the patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left side elevated to assist with gravity during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral attachments of the colon, transitioning to a supramusicalic approach to preserve the omentum. The gastrocolic ligament is left intact, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the remaining lineal colic and renal colic ligaments.

The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal margins for resection after freeing the splenic flexure. A small hole is made in the mesentery, revealing the prominent blood supply, specifically the left branch of the middle colic artery. The surgeon widens the hole in the mesentery to facilitate the resection.

The surgeon selects the distal and proximal margins of the colon, ensuring preservation of the ascending branch of the left colic artery visible below the ligatures. The mesentery is carefully dissected up to the proximal margin, with visible pulsatile blood flow reassuring the adequacy of the blood supply. The proximal bowel appears slightly more dilated than the distal bowel, and the stapler is positioned in preparation for checking ICG.

The surgeon uses two firings of the stapler to transect the proximal bowel, which appears dilated. The stapler is then positioned to staple just distal to the stricture, where the bowel caliber is visibly less dilated. The surgeon lines up an isoparastaltic anastomosis, placing stay sutures and making a colotomy on both the descending and transverse colon.

The surgeon uses a gold vascular load stapler through the suprapubic port to create a wide common channel anastomosis. The camera is positioned at the suprapubic location to optimize the angles for suturing. Hemostasis is checked visually, and the anastomosis is sewn closed using a three OB lock suture, with a stay suture pulled through the anterior abdominal wall to maintain position.

The surgeon continues suturing around the near corner of the anastomosis, using a transition stitch to close the anterior wall. The camera is positioned umbilically, and suturing is performed from the left upper quadrant and suprapubic port. The surgeon meticulously closes the lip of the anastomosis and imbricates back down to ensure complete coverage of the initial closure site.

The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to confirm adequate blood supply to the completed anastomosis, which is visually inspected to ensure it lays without tension. The omentum is draped over the anastomosis for coverage, and the specimen is prepared for extraction through the suprapubic port.
